fr:title.text: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f\colon E\to B is surjective is a proposition.
By .

fr:title.text: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If A is a proposition and B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} is family of propositions, then the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx} is a proposition.

If A is contractible and B\colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is a family of contractible types, then the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is contractible.

Let A be n-truncated and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and . In the inductive step, we need to show that for all u,v\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type u=v is n+1-truncated assuming the same of all the identification types of A and Bx for x:A. By , u=v is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} p_*u.2=v.2 and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis; thus the result follows from .

Let A be an arbitrary type and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and respectively. In the inductive step, we need to show that for all f,g\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type f=g is n+1-truncated assuming the same for the identification types of each Bx. By , f=g is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis. Therefore, the result follows from .
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from and .

fr:title.text: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.

To identify two witnesses \phi ,\phi ' \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}x = y that A is a proposition, it suffices via to exhibit a double homotopy in A identifying each \phi xy with \phi 'xy. By , this follows from our assumption that A is a proposition.
It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

fr:ol.fr:li: When n=-2, we recall from that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from that being a proposition is a proposition.

For n \geq 0, we assume by induction that being n-1-truncated is a proposition to prove that being n-truncated is a proposition. In other words, we must show that \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {isTruncated}\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen n-1\right \rparen \mathclose {} \,A is a proposition. By our inductive hypothesis, this is a dependent product of propositions ().

fr:title.text: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f\colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

We must check that the product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {isTruncated}\,n\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {fiber}\,f\,x\right \rparen \mathclose {} is a proposition; indeed, this is a product of propositions () as being an n-truncated type is a proposition ().

fr:title.text: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: That a given map f\colon E\to B is surjective is a proposition.
By .
That a given map f\colon A \to B is bijective is a proposition.
By , via .
A function f\colon A\to B is called surjective when for any b\colon B, the fiber of f over b is inhabited.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx}, it suffices to show any other g :\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is contractible with centre f.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
